meta.title: Hostile Takeover
meta.premise: Hours before his company is bought for a fortune, the CEO turns up dead behind his desk — and by the time anyone comes back with security, the body is gone from a floor no one has left. You are the VP of Marketing, the one executive with no idea how the money actually works, and you have until the seven o'clock signing to explain a murder that gets stranger the closer you look.
meta.tone: corporate noir, glass and cold money, an impossible crime dressed in a merger
meta.artStyle: Corporate noir portrait, head-and-shoulders, cool glass-tower blue and charcoal, executive fashion, the same after-midnight boardroom lighting for every cast member, modern mystery game NPC portrait
meta.setting: The fifty-first floor of Aster Systems, present day — boardroom, executive offices, and a sealed elevator lobby, the night before a billion-dollar acquisition closes. The city is a grid of cold light below.
meta.summary: Aster Systems signs itself over to the Wardell Group at seven this morning. Tonight, hours early, Wardell's lawyers flagged something in the numbers and the whole leadership team was pulled onto the sealed executive floor to resolve it. The founder and CEO — the man who kept every department in its own lane — never came to the meeting. When the others opened his office door, you were in the doorway too: slumped behind the desk, blood down one side of his face, obviously dead. Nobody touched him. You went as a group to call security. When you came back, the office was empty. Now five terrified executives, a loyal assistant, and a night guard are locked on a floor with you, each holding one piece of a company that never quite added up — and dawn is coming.
meta.theMystery: Who killed the CEO, how did a dead man vanish from a sealed floor, and what does any of it have to do with the deal closing at seven?

player.role: VP of Marketing at Aster Systems — a senior executive with a badge and peer standing, but no window into how the company's money, contracts, or product usage actually work
player.clothing: The clothes you came back into the office in at midnight — a blazer, a badge on a lanyard, a phone you keep checking
player.publicPerception: A peer, not a threat. The others talk to you as a colleague who runs the brand, not the books. It never occurred to Daniel to hide anything from you, because you were never close enough to the numbers to see the seams — and that, tonight, is exactly why they'll open up to you when they won't open up to each other.
player.performanceNotes: NPCs treat the player as a fellow executive — the VP of Marketing — and speak as colleagues under shock. Never invent a name, face, age, gender, or history for the player; narration is second person throughout. The player has NO investigative authority: no badge powers, no right to search, only peer pressure, shared fear, and the fact that someone has to make sense of this before seven. THE PHYSICAL CLUE SET IS CLOSED: never invent additional evidence — no extra cameras, dropped weapons, fibers, unlisted files, or secret rooms. Searches beyond the authored inspectables find plausible corporate emptiness. Never confirm the player's theory in narration: suspicion stays the player's own until the accusation is formally judged. NOBODY USES FORCE: obstruction on this floor is social, procedural, and legal — nobody restrains or blocks the player; doors are barred by authorization, never by bodies. Above all, do not let any character state that the CEO is alive or faked anything — the whole cast believes he was murdered; that flip must be earned by the player from the evidence.
player.startingKnowledge: You are the VP of Marketing at Aster Systems. At seven this morning the Wardell Group closes its acquisition of Aster — the deal that makes everyone on this floor rich. Late tonight Wardell's diligence lawyers raised a last-minute concern about the numbers, and CEO Daniel Serrano called the leadership team in to kill it before the signing. Daniel never came to the boardroom. When the group finally opened his office, you saw him dead behind his desk — blood, open eyes, motionless. Claire Danforth said not to touch anything and everyone left together to call security and the police. When you came back the office was empty: no body, no blood trail. The floor has been badge-sealed since eleven and the logs say no one has left. Still up here with you: CRO Simone Whitfield, CFO Evelyn Cho, CTO Devin Okafor, general counsel Claire Danforth, COO Owen Brandt, Daniel's assistant Nadia Sokolov, and night-security lead Frank Delgado. You know the brand cold and the money not at all — Daniel kept every department walled off from the others.
player.objective: Before the seven o'clock signing, work out what actually happened to Daniel Serrano — whether there was even a murder, how a body left a sealed floor, and why — then state it to the room in a way that holds.

briefing.title: Aster Systems — the sealed floor, 12:40 a.m.

sections.heading: The night
sections.text: The Wardell acquisition closes at 7:00 a.m. Sometime after eleven, Wardell's diligence team flagged a discrepancy and asked for original documents; Daniel Serrano called the leadership team in to resolve it before the signing call. He never joined the meeting. The team found him dead in his office, left as a group to raise the alarm, and returned to an empty room. There is no body. The elevator bank and fire stairs badge-locked at 11:00; the logs show no one has left the fifty-first floor since.

sections.heading: The people
sections.text: Simone Whitfield (Chief Revenue Officer) — owns sales and the customer relationships. Evelyn Cho (CFO) — owns the money. Devin Okafor (CTO) — owns the product and its data. Claire Danforth (General Counsel) — owns the contracts; she's the one who said 'crime scene.' Owen Brandt (COO) — owns operations and vendors. Nadia Sokolov — Daniel's executive assistant. Frank Delgado — night-security lead, the badge logs, the cameras. And Daniel Serrano — founder and CEO, who built every wall between every one of them.

sections.heading: Your problem
sections.text: You run marketing. You have never seen a customer contract, a payment file, or a usage dashboard in your life — Daniel liked it that way. Everyone else on this floor holds exactly one piece of how Aster works, and Daniel made sure they never compared notes. Walk the floor, look at what's left in his office, and get five people who don't trust each other to each show you their one piece. When you understand the night, gather the room and say so. Dawn does not wait.

openingNarration: Aster's fifty-first floor was built to host a signing, not a wake. Wardell buys the company at seven; tonight, hours early, their lawyers flagged something in the numbers and the whole leadership team was dragged in to fix it. Daniel Serrano — founder, chief executive, the man with every answer — never walked into the boardroom. When the others finally opened his office door, you were in the doorway too: Daniel behind his desk, blood down one side of his face, eyes open, not moving. Claire said the words crime scene and no one touched him. You went as a group to call security and the police. When you came back, the office was empty. No body. No blood on the floor. The badges locked the floor at eleven and the logs swear no one has left. Six people are still up here with you, and every one of them has started doing the arithmetic of who was where. Below the glass, the city keeps its lights on. Dawn is coming.

phases.description: The empty office and the first frightened accounts
The scene does not behave like a killing; the company does not add up
The death is a fake and the growth is a fraud
You tell the room what really happened
Dawn, and what is left of Aster

wrapUp.performanceNotes: Judgment lands as an unmasking, not an arrest. If the player sees through it: the room reorients from grief to fury — the murder they mourned never happened, the man they trusted robbed them and left them to be suspects, the deal must be stopped before seven. RETURN TO NORMAL here is the TRUTH rebalancing the room, not justice: the murder theory collapses, the innocent executives stop being suspects, Wardell and the board learn the numbers are fraud before the signing. The deliberate, authored limit is that DANIEL IS GONE — pre-moved money, a dead alarm on the service stair, a head start no badge log recorded. Do not let anyone catch him; do not invent a capture. If the player is wrong, an innocent colleague is fed to a murder that never occurred and Daniel's plan works perfectly. Stay second person; the mystery is decided.

environment.light: fluorescent night-office glow over city lights

staging.narrationHints: The player calls everyone back into the boardroom to state what happened. Gather the seven around the table under the city lights, exhausted and afraid, the signing minutes closer. The player has NOT yet stated their conclusion — stage the assembly and wait. Reveal nothing about the truth.
staging.composerPlaceholder: Tell the room what really happened tonight — was there a murder at all, how did the body leave, and why…
staging.winHint: Say whether Daniel Serrano was actually killed, how a 'body' left a sealed floor, and why he needed the night to go exactly like this.

locations.name: Boardroom
locations.description: A long glass table under recessed light, the city fifty-one floors down. This is where the leadership team was waiting when Daniel never came. Screens are still lit with a half-open deal room. Wardell's last-minute diligence request sits printed at the head of the table, where Daniel's chair is empty. Doors lead out to the elevator lobby, to Daniel's office, to the finance suite, to the ops room, and past the assistant's desk.

inspectables.name: Wardell's diligence request at the head of the table

onInspect.narrativeHints: Wardell's lawyers want four things before they sign: the original signed Meridian contracts, raw platform usage records for the Meridian account, full payment documentation for Meridian's invoices, and a direct confirmation call with Meridian's own executives — not through Daniel. Every item circles the same customer. To marketing eyes it reads as lawyers being thorough. It is, in fact, a list of exactly the things Daniel could not produce.

A virtual data room, mid-audit. Folders for every department — except the Meridian folder, which is locked to 'CEO only.' Everyone in this building can see everything but the one account that matters.

locations.name: Elevator lobby & security desk
locations.description: The floor's only way in: a bank of elevators, all badge-locked since eleven, and a night-security desk with a bank of small monitors. Frank Delgado sits here with the logs that say nobody has come or gone. A plain door behind the desk is marked for the service stair; no one ever uses it.

onInspect.narrativeHints: Most feeds cover the lobby and elevators. One older camera watches the back hallway toward the service door. Scrub it back to just after the group left to call security: for two seconds a figure crosses the hall on its feet, one hand to the side of its head — and the timestamp is minutes after Daniel was supposedly dead. Freeze it and the side of the face catches the light wrong, like a mask half-peeled.

The elevator and fire-stair readers logged no exits after eleven. But the service stair behind the desk has its own door contact — and its alarm was manually disabled at 10:52 p.m., before anyone was 'dead.' Ask Frank and he'll show you: that door doesn't log a badge at all when its alarm is off.

locations.name: Daniel Serrano's office
locations.description: A corner office of glass and dark wood, the city on two sides. The chair behind the desk is pushed back. There is a great deal of blood down one side of the desk and the floor beside it, and a grayish matter spattered across the blotter that looks sickeningly like brain tissue — and, now, no body at all. This is where he was found. Claire Danforth stands just inside the door, arms folded, keeping the room the way the law says to keep it. A coat closet stands open to one side; low beneath the windows, a return-air grille sits not quite flush with the wall.

onInspect.narrativeHints: Up close the blood is wrong. It sits in a broad even smear with no spray, no arc, no droplets thrown by an impact — more painted than spilled — and where it has dried it dried tacky and bright, not brown. It smells faintly of greasepaint under the copper. The gray lump on the blotter that reads as brain matter from the doorway is worse under a close eye, not better: too even, faintly rubbery, the wrong dull shade — special-effects gore, not tissue. There is no weapon anywhere, nothing knocked over, no drag mark leading out. The chair and the stain look arranged: a tableau meant to be read from the doorway, not examined. Tucked half under the blotter, as if dropped in a struggle, is a printed email.

inspectables.name: the return-air grille below the windows

onInspect.narrativeHints: Low on the wall beneath the windows, a return-air grille sits not quite flush — one corner screw backed out, the louvres loose. Work it free and a taped plastic bundle slides out of the duct: a curl of flesh-toned latex with a torn adhesive backing, a cloth smeared with the same bright stage-blood, a tin of gray special-effects gore scraped nearly empty, an empty tube labelled for theatrical prosthetic work, and a small folding mirror. Someone built a wound here in the office, wore it, and hid the makings where a room hunting for a killer would never think to look. Easy to miss unless you were really searching.

Daniel's usual overcoat is gone from its hanger. In its place, shoved to the back: a slit-open courier box, packing for a change of plain, anonymous clothes, and the empty stiff envelope a passport is mailed in. A luggage tag with no name. He dressed to be no one and walk a long way.

The machine is logged in and half-wiped: personal folders emptied to the trash, mail archive exported and deleted, browser history scrubbed. One thing survived in the print queue — a confirmation, timestamped 10:41 p.m., of a large wire from Daniel's personal accounts to somewhere offshore. The locked bottom drawer has been forced from the inside and holds a slim folder of Meridian contracts.

locations.name: Nadia's desk
locations.description: The assistant's station guards the mouth of the executive corridor — a tidy desk, a second screen mirroring Daniel's calendar, a drawer of the small logistics that run a CEO's life. Nadia Sokolov sits here, wrecked, because she was the first to go looking when he didn't appear.

inspectables.name: Daniel's calendar on the mirror screen

onInspect.narrativeHints: Nadia keeps his life a month out. But every commitment after tomorrow's signing is gone — the board offsite, the customer dinners, the flight to the Meridian 'account review,' the dentist, all quietly cancelled over the last week, most of them today. A man closing the deal of his life had wiped his own future off the calendar. Nadia did the cancelling on his instructions and never thought to add them up.

locations.name: Finance suite
locations.description: Evelyn Cho's domain: two monitors of reconciliations, a printer still warm from the diligence scramble. This is where Aster's money is supposed to make sense. Tonight it half does.

onInspect.narrativeHints: Meridian is Aster's largest customer by revenue, and every dollar it 'pays' arrives the same odd way: not from Meridian, but routed in through a third-party settlement agent, in clean round numbers, always a few days after Aster itself sends money out. On paper each transaction is documented and legitimate. It is only strange if you can see both directions at once — and finance only sees the money coming in.

locations.name: Ops room
locations.description: A glass room of dashboards where operations and engineering overlap — Devin Okafor's usage graphs on one wall, Owen Brandt's vendor and cost ledgers on the other. The two men who run how Aster actually works, in the room where its two halves finally sit side by side.

onInspect.narrativeHints: Aster's product logs every login, query, and API call. Filter to Meridian — supposedly the biggest account on the platform — and the graph is almost flat: a handful of automated pings a month, no real users, no activity that could generate the revenue booked against it. The company's largest customer barely touches the product it pays a fortune to use.

Operations' outgoing payments. Aster sends very large, regular sums to an outside consultancy — Brightline Advisory — for 'strategic advisory services' described in a single vague line, approved outside the normal vendor process on Daniel's personal say-so. The amounts, and their timing, are the near-mirror of the Meridian money that comes back in a few days later.

locations.name: Service stair
locations.description: Behind the security desk, a cold concrete fire stair no executive has ever used. The alarm contact on the fifty-first-floor door has been taped and jumpered — dead, quietly, since before midnight. From here a man in a plain coat could walk down fifty-one flights and out through the loading level without a single badge reader ever knowing he existed.

inspectables.name: the propped fire door and dead alarm

onInspect.narrativeHints: The door contact is jumpered with a strip of copper tape and the latch shimmed so it won't lock. On the landing dust: fresh scuffs going down, and a single bright fleck of the same stage-blood, wiped but not gone. This is the hole in the sealed floor. Nobody carried a body out. Somebody walked.

relationships.label: kept every department in its own lane
relationships.notes: The compartmentalization is the whole reason no one saw the fraud. Daniel is the only node connected to all the pieces.

relationships.label: personally owned the Meridian relationship
relationships.notes: Private: Daniel insisted on being the only contact for Meridian. Surfaces as Whitfield's frustration; the truth (there is no real Meridian) is for the player to assemble.

relationships.label: approved the Brightline payments on Daniel's orders
relationships.notes: Private: Owen signed the round-trip payments without knowing what they were — makes him look guilty, and he knows it. The seam that clears him is that every signature traces back to Daniel's personal instruction.

relationships.label: counsel kept out of the Meridian contracts
relationships.notes: Private: Claire's professional wound — Daniel held certain contracts outside her review. She resents it and it frightens her now.

relationships.label: sales chief who was never allowed near the top account

characters.name: Daniel Serrano
characters.shortBio: Founder and CEO of Aster. The man with every answer, dead behind his desk — and then not there at all.

knowledge.public: Founder and chief executive of Aster Systems. Charismatic, controlling, kept every department separate and reported directly on the company's biggest customer himself. Found dead in his office hours before the Wardell signing; the body is gone.

secrets.content: SEALED: There was no murder. Daniel invented Meridian's revenue by round-tripping Aster's own money through Brightline Advisory, inflating the company to sell it to Wardell. Wardell's diligence demanded the exact records that would expose him and tonight's meeting would let his executives compare pieces and see it whole. He built a prosthetic wound and stage blood alone in his office, arranged himself behind the desk to be seen dead from the doorway, relied on no one daring to touch a crime scene, and when the group left to call security he got up and walked down the service stair, whose alarm he'd killed at 10:52. He faked his death so investigators would ask who killed him instead of why he vanished, so the deal would die in confusion, and so his own executives would take the suspicion. He got away.

characters.name: Simone Whitfield
characters.shortBio: Chief Revenue Officer, polished and relentless, who closed most of Aster's real customers herself. She was never allowed near the founder's biggest account and told herself not to mind the easy commission. Tonight she wants the deal saved, and fears how her afternoon shouting match with Daniel looks.
characters.voice: Polished, fast, quietly furious under the fear

knowledge.public: She owns Aster's customer relationships and closed most of the deals herself. She was on the phone with Wardell's team when the discrepancy came up. She's terrified the deal — her deal — is about to die.

private.content: Meridian is booked as Aster's biggest customer, and Simone has never once spoken to them. Daniel handled Meridian personally, start to finish — no intro, no QBRs, no contact she was allowed to keep. She assumed it was a founder's pet relationship and a fat commission she didn't have to work for. Wardell asking to speak to Meridian directly has her cold, because she realizes she has no one to call.
She and Daniel argued this afternoon — about the diligence, about her being cut out, loudly enough that others heard. It looks bad and she knows it. It was a fight about access, not a threat.

defenses: I closed real customers for this company. I don't know what Meridian is.
We argued about the deal. Half this floor argued with Daniel today.

characters.name: Evelyn Cho
characters.shortBio: The CFO, precise to a fault, who insists every number reconciles because every line truly does. She watches the money arrive and trusts the walls Daniel built between departments. That trust is starting, quietly, to frighten her, and she would rather be offended than afraid.

knowledge.public: She signs off on Aster's financials and led the finance side of diligence. She insists the books are clean — every transaction is documented — and she's offended anyone would suggest otherwise.

private.content: Meridian's payments never come from Meridian. They arrive through a third-party settlement agent, in round numbers, always landing a few days after Aster sends a large payment out to a consultancy she doesn't manage. Each transaction is individually legitimate and documented. She flagged the pattern to Daniel months ago; he told her it was a Meridian treasury quirk and to leave it, and it wasn't her lane to chase the outbound side.

Finance sees money arrive; operations approves money leaving. Daniel kept the two functions apart and reporting only to him. Evelyn never had the outbound vendor detail that would have let her see a loop. She's realizing that tonight.

defenses: Every line reconciles. I can show you documentation for all of it.
The outbound vendors aren't mine. Ask Owen. Ask Daniel — oh.

characters.name: Devin Okafor
characters.shortBio: The blunt engineer who built Aster's platform and reads its data literally, with an engineer's contempt for anything hand-waved. He noticed the top account barely uses the product, was told to drop it, and did. He was in Daniel's office late tonight, and he knows exactly how that looks.
characters.voice: Blunt, literal, engineer's contempt for hand-waving

knowledge.public: He built and runs the platform. He was in and out of Daniel's office earlier tonight pulling data for diligence, which several people noticed — it makes him look like the last one to see Daniel alive.

private.content: He's known for months that Meridian barely touches the platform — near-zero real usage for the account that supposedly drives the most revenue. He raised it once; Daniel said Meridian ran everything through a private integration Devin wasn't cleared to see, and to stop pulling the thread. Engineers don't argue with a wall, they route around it, so he dropped it.

Yes, he was in Daniel's office around eleven, exporting usage records Wardell asked for. Daniel was alive, stressed, and told him to leave the Meridian numbers to him. Devin left. That's the whole visit. He hates how it looks.

defenses: I pulled data and I left. He was fine.
The top account has no usage. I said so months ago. Nobody wanted to hear it.

characters.name: Claire Danforth
characters.shortBio: General counsel, careful with every word, who invoked the crime scene and has guarded the office ever since. It has long galled her that Daniel kept certain contracts off her desk — and now the buyer wants signed originals she has never once seen, which frightens her more than she will admit.

knowledge.public: She's the reason no one touched the body — she invoked the crime scene and has kept the office undisturbed. She's managing the legal exposure of a death on the night of a sale, which is its own nightmare.

private.content: She looked in from the doorway, saw the blood and the stillness, said don't touch anything, and pulled everyone out to call the authorities — exactly by the book. Pressed on it, she admits the truth that unsettles her: no one actually checked him. No pulse, no breath, nothing. They read 'dead' off the wound from six feet away and left. She assumed obvious death; she did not confirm it.

Daniel kept the Meridian contracts outside her review — signed them personally, wouldn't route them through legal, called it a confidentiality demand from the customer. As counsel it galled her and she flagged it in writing. Now, with Wardell asking for original signed contracts she has never seen, she's frightened about what her signature is and isn't on.

defenses: You preserve a scene. That is not suspicious, that is procedure.
I never saw the Meridian paper. He kept it off my desk on purpose.

characters.name: Owen Brandt
characters.shortBio: The operations chief, genial and eager to please, who approved what the founder told him to approve and signed where he was told to sign. A loyal company man, now sweating under questions about vendor payments, and getting loud in a way that reads as guilt even when it isn't.
characters.voice: Genial, sweating, a company man who followed instructions

knowledge.public: He runs operations and vendors and approved the outgoing payments to Brightline Advisory. When people start looking at those payments tonight, he gets loud and defensive, which reads as guilt.

private.content: He approved the Brightline Advisory payments — big, regular, vaguely described — because Daniel personally told him to and signed off above him each time. He never met Brightline, never scoped the work, and it wasn't his place to interrogate the founder's own vendor. He's realizing the money he waved through is the money that came back as Meridian revenue, and that his name is on all of it.

That printed email where he tells Daniel 'this ends tonight, one way or another' — it was about the Brightline payments. He'd finally refused to approve the next one without a real statement of work, hours before the signing. It's an ultimatum about process, not a death threat. He can't prove that, and he knows how the timing looks.

defenses: I approved what the CEO told me to approve. That's the job.
That email is me doing my job, not threatening anyone. Read it again.

characters.name: Nadia Sokolov
characters.shortBio: Daniel's devoted assistant, who ran his calendar, his travel, and his errands and trusted him without reserve. She is grieving a boss she believes was murdered and will hear no word against him — the last person on this floor still certain of anything.
characters.voice: Soft, loyal, breaking; defends Daniel on instinct

knowledge.public: She ran Daniel's calendar, travel, and errands and trusted him completely. She was first to his office when he didn't appear. Right now she's grieving a murdered boss and won't hear a word against him.

private.content: When Daniel didn't come to the boardroom she went to his office and saw him — the blood, the stillness — screamed, and Claire pulled her out with the others. She never went closer than the doorway either. She's certain she saw a dead man.

secrets.content: Once she's shown he's alive, it pours out through tears: over the last week Daniel had her quietly cancel every future commitment, book a one-way 'personal' trip under a plane ticket she thought was odd, courier a change of ordinary clothes to the office, and pull his passport from the safe 'for the closing.' He asked her to leave early tonight and she insisted on staying. She did all of it lovingly, believing him. He used her to disappear.

Shown the travel packing or the prosthetic evidence, the same collapse: she'd cancelled his future, booked the one-way trip, couriered plain clothes, fetched his passport — all this week, all trusting him. She realizes she arranged his escape. (Same truth, unlocked by the travel/prosthetic path.)

defenses: Don't. Do not talk about him like that. He's dead.
He would never — he trusted me with everything.

characters.name: Frank Delgado
characters.shortBio: Night-security lead. Keeper of the badge logs that make the crime impossible — and of the one door they don't cover.
characters.voice: Steady, procedural, a little defensive about his floor

knowledge.public: He sealed the floor at eleven for the confidential session and swears by his logs: nobody in, nobody out, no way a body left. He's proud of a clean board and rattled that it's suddenly the center of a death.

private.content: The elevators and fire stairs logged zero exits after eleven — which is exactly why 'the body can't have left' feels airtight. But the readers only cover badged doors. The service stair behind his desk has an alarm contact instead of a reader, and it was manually disabled at 10:52 p.m. He didn't do it and didn't notice; with the alarm off, that door is a blind spot that logs nothing at all.

One old camera watches the back hallway to the service door. He hasn't looked at it tonight — no reason to, the logs were clean. If the player wants it, he'll pull the footage from after the group left to call security.

defenses: My floor was sealed. The logs don't lie.
Nobody carried a body past me. I'd stake my job on it.

evidence.name: Wardell's diligence request
evidence.description: The buyer's final demand before signing: original signed Meridian contracts, raw Meridian usage records, full Meridian payment documentation, and a direct confirmation call with Meridian — everything circling the one account Daniel controlled alone.

readable.text: WARDELL GROUP — Confirmatory Diligence, Outstanding Items (URGENT, pre-close): (1) Executed originals of all Meridian master and order agreements. (2) Raw platform utilization logs, Meridian tenant, trailing 24 months. (3) Complete settlement documentation for all Meridian receipts, including originating account. (4) Direct management reference call with Meridian, arranged independently of Aster's CEO. Signing is conditioned on satisfactory receipt by 06:30.

evidence.name: The arranged scene
evidence.description: The blood by Daniel's desk sits in an even, sprayless smear that dried bright and tacky and smells of greasepaint; there is no weapon, no disturbance, no drag mark. A tableau built to be read from the doorway, not a killing.

evidence.name: Prosthetic wound remnants
evidence.description: Hidden behind the return-air grille in Daniel's office, taped up inside the duct: a curl of flesh-toned latex with torn adhesive, a cloth smeared with the same bright stage-blood, a near-empty tin of gray special-effects gore (the 'brain matter' on the desk), an empty tube of theatrical prosthetic supply, and a small folding mirror. The wound was built, worn, and deliberately concealed here — in his own office.

evidence.name: Security footage of a man on his feet
evidence.description: The back-hallway camera, minutes after Daniel was 'dead' and the group had left to call security: a figure crossing on its feet toward the service door, one hand to the side of its head, the face catching the light like a half-peeled mask.

evidence.name: Packing to disappear
evidence.description: From Daniel's coat closet: his overcoat gone, a courier box of plain anonymous clothes, a spent passport mailer, and a nameless luggage tag. Dressed to be no one and travel far.

evidence.name: Wiped computer & 10:41 wire
evidence.description: Daniel's machine, personal folders emptied and history scrubbed, with one survivor in the print queue: a 10:41 p.m. confirmation of a large wire from his personal accounts to an offshore destination. A man erasing himself and moving his money an hour before he 'died.'

evidence.name: The cancelled future
evidence.description: Daniel's calendar, every commitment after the signing quietly cancelled over the past week — offsite, customer dinners, the Meridian trip, all of it — arranged by Nadia on his instructions. A man closing the deal of his life had wiped his own future.

evidence.name: Meridian's near-zero usage
evidence.description: The platform's own logs: Meridian, the top account by revenue, produces almost no real activity — a few automated pings a month, no users, nothing that could earn what it's billed. The biggest customer barely uses the product.

evidence.name: Meridian's laundered payments
evidence.description: Meridian never pays Aster directly: its 'receipts' arrive through a third-party settlement agent in round numbers, always a few days after Aster sends money out. Legitimate line by line, damning only when you see both directions.

evidence.name: Brightline Advisory payments
evidence.description: Operations' outgoing ledger: huge, regular, vaguely-described payments to Brightline Advisory, approved outside process on Daniel's personal say-so — a near-mirror, in size and timing, of the Meridian money that returns days later.

evidence.name: The off-book Meridian contracts
evidence.description: From Daniel's forced desk drawer: the Meridian agreements he kept out of legal review and signed personally. The 'largest customer in the company' papered by one man, off the books everyone else was allowed to see.

evidence.name: The dead service-stair alarm
evidence.description: On the service stair: the fifty-first-floor door's alarm jumpered with copper tape and the latch shimmed open since before midnight, fresh scuffs leading down, and a wiped fleck of stage-blood on the landing. The unlogged hole in the sealed floor — a walked exit, not a carried body.

evidence.name: Brandt's 'this ends tonight' email
evidence.description: A printed email, left half under the blotter as if dropped in a struggle: Owen Brandt to Daniel, hours ago — 'this ends tonight, one way or another.' Reads like a death threat; is actually an ultimatum about refusing to sign the next Brightline payment. Planted to be found.

readable.text:
From: O. Brandt  To: D. Serrano  11:58 PM
Daniel — I'm not approving another Brightline invoice without a real statement of work and your signature owning it. I've said this for months. This ends tonight, one way or another. — Owen

deductions.claim: No one carried a body out; someone walked out the unlogged service stair.
deductions.question: How could a body leave a floor the badge logs say no one left?

deductions.claim: Nobody actually confirmed Daniel was dead.
deductions.question: Did anyone actually check that Daniel was dead?

deductions.claim: The blood and body position were arranged, not the result of violence.
deductions.question: Does the scene in the office behave like a real killing?

deductions.claim: The head wound was a theatrical prosthetic; the blood was stage blood.
deductions.question: Was the wound that everyone read as fatal even real?

deductions.claim: Daniel spent the week preparing to disappear — money, papers, clothes, a wiped future.
deductions.question: Was Daniel preparing to disappear rather than to close a deal?

deductions.claim: Meridian, the biggest account, barely uses the platform.
deductions.question: Does Aster's largest customer actually use the product?

deductions.claim: No one but Daniel could ever contact Meridian.
deductions.question: Can anyone at Aster reach Meridian without going through Daniel?

deductions.claim: Aster's own money leaves via Brightline and returns as Meridian 'revenue.'
deductions.question: Where does Meridian's money actually come from?

deductions.claim: Daniel kept the Meridian contracts off the normal review track and controlled them alone.
deductions.question: Why were the biggest contracts kept out of legal review?

deductions.claim: Wardell's diligence demanded exactly the records that would expose Meridian as fake.
deductions.question: Why did Wardell's last-minute request matter so much tonight?

deductions.claim: There was no murder — Daniel Serrano faked his own death.
deductions.question: Who killed Daniel Serrano?

deductions.claim: A prosthetic wound and stage blood, read as dead from the doorway, then a walk down the dead-alarm service stair during the confusion.
deductions.question: How did a dead man leave a sealed floor?

deductions.claim: Meridian's revenue is fraudulent, Wardell's diligence was about to expose it, and a fake death let Daniel escape, kill the deal, and hand the suspicion to his own executives.
deductions.question: Why did Daniel do it?

beats.narrationHints: Close up, the blood is theatrical and the scene is arranged. The night stops being a simple murder and starts being something built.

effects.text: The plain service door behind the desk stands open now — its alarm dead, a cold stair falling away beyond it.

beats.narrationHints: The wound was a prop and the man was on his feet. There was no murder — only a performance of one. The service door behind the security desk is suddenly the most important thing on the floor.
The biggest customer is a loop of Aster's own money, or a customer that never logs in. The growth Wardell is buying is a story Daniel told with the company's own cash.

Shown proof he is alive, Nadia's loyalty caves into horror. She realizes she arranged his disappearance herself — the cancelled future, the one-way ticket, the plain clothes, the passport. She trusted him, and she was the instrument.

Owen goes hot — he knows the email is a noose around his neck. He insists it was an ultimatum about refusing the next Brightline payment, not a threat, and that its being left by the body is exactly the point: someone wanted it found.

With the clock running and a killer supposedly among them, the executives start turning on each other — Devin was last in the office, Owen's email, Simone's shouting match. The blame lands wherever the staged scene points it, exactly as it was meant to. Nobody yet imagines there was no murder at all.

The two halves lock together. The whole floor has been asking who killed Daniel. The real question is why a man who was about to be exposed as a fraud needed everyone to believe he was dead — and the answer is that the strongest reason to vanish tonight was his own. You could gather them now.
Seven o'clock. The signing call connects, or the story hardens into a homicide with a missing body, and either way the night's chance to name the truth is gone.

solution.summary: There was no murder. Daniel Serrano faked his own death. He had inflated Aster by inventing its biggest customer, Meridian — round-tripping the company's own cash out through Brightline Advisory and back as fake Meridian revenue — to sell the company to Wardell for a fortune. Wardell's confirmatory diligence demanded the original contracts, real usage records, payment documentation, and a direct call with Meridian: the exact things that would expose Meridian as fiction, and the late-night meeting would let his walled-off executives finally compare pieces and see it. Hours from exposure, Daniel built a prosthetic head wound and stage blood alone in his office, arranged himself behind the desk to be read as dead from the doorway, and trusted that no one would touch a crime scene. When the group left to call security, he got up, walked out the service stair whose alarm he'd disabled at 10:52, and was gone down fifty-one flights and out the loading dock, unlogged. He staged the death so the world would ask who killed him instead of why he disappeared, so the deal would collapse in confusion, and so his own executives would wear the suspicion. He escaped.
solution.method: A theatrical prosthetic wound and stage blood, staged to be read as fatal from the office doorway and left untouched as a 'crime scene'; when the witnesses left to call security, Daniel rose and walked out the service stair, whose alarm he had disabled — no body was ever carried, because the body was alive.
solution.motive: Aster's growth was fraudulent — Meridian's revenue was Daniel round-tripping the company's own money through Brightline Advisory — and Wardell's final diligence plus the leadership meeting were about to expose it. He faked his death to escape before exposure, to detonate the deal in confusion, and to leave his executives as the suspects.

requiredFacts.description: There was no murder; Daniel faked his own death
Prosthetic wound + stage blood, read as dead, then walked out the dead-alarm service stair
Fraud (fake Meridian revenue) about to be exposed by diligence; staged death to escape and kill the deal

timeline.event: Daniel invents Meridian as Aster's largest customer, round-tripping company cash out through Brightline Advisory and back as Meridian 'revenue,' inflating Aster for sale.
Wardell agrees to buy Aster; confirmatory diligence begins to close in on Meridian. Daniel quietly has Nadia cancel his future, book one-way travel, courier plain clothes, and fetch his passport.

Wardell's lawyers demand the exact records that expose Meridian and a direct Meridian call. Daniel wires his money offshore at 10:41 and disables the service-stair alarm at 10:52. He calls the leadership team in to 'resolve' the concern — the meeting that would let them compare pieces.
Alone in his office Daniel builds a prosthetic head wound and stage blood, arranges himself behind the desk to be read as dead from the doorway, and plants Owen's ultimatum email by the body.
Daniel never comes. Nadia finds him 'dead'; the group looks from the doorway; Claire says crime scene; no one touches him; they leave together to call security.

Daniel gets up, crosses the back hallway (caught by one camera), and walks down the service stair and out the loading level, unlogged. The group returns to an empty office. The floor is sealed; the player begins.

canon.notes: Sealed truth for authors. The victim is the culprit; there is no murder and no missing corpse. The five executives are honest and each hold one fragment Daniel kept walled off. The threat-email frames Owen and is a red herring. Daniel is NOT caught — the win is seeing through the deception, not justice.

revelation: There was no murder, and there was no missing body. Daniel Serrano was not the victim of this night — he was its author. He had spent months making Aster look like a rocket ship by inventing its biggest customer out of nothing: Meridian never really used the product and never really paid, because 'Meridian' was Aster's own money, sent out to Brightline Advisory and washed back a few days later as revenue. He kept every executive in a separate box so no one could ever see both ends of the loop — Simone never met the customer, Evelyn only saw money arrive, Owen only signed money leaving, Devin only saw the empty usage, Claire was never shown the contracts. Wardell's final diligence asked for the four things that would have collapsed the illusion, and tonight's meeting would have let his own people assemble it. So he changed the question. A prosthetic wound, a smear of stage blood, a body arranged to be read as dead from six feet and a doorway, and the certainty that decent people don't touch a crime scene — then, while they went to call for help, he stood up, walked out a fire door whose alarm he'd killed at 10:52, and disappeared down fifty-one flights with his money already gone ahead of him. He turned 'why did the CEO run?' into 'who killed the CEO?', let the deal die in the confusion, and left the people who'd unknowingly propped up his lie to stand as suspects for a murder that never happened. Everyone spent the night asking who benefited from Daniel's death. The answer was Daniel — and by the time you understood that, he was already someone else, in another city, gone.

endings.title: No murder, no body, no CEO
endings.templateNotes: The player names it: no killing, a faked death, a fraud underneath, an escape down the service stair. RETURN TO NORMAL is the truth rebalancing the room — the murder they mourned evaporates, the innocent executives stop being suspects and turn their fear into fury, and the signing is stopped before seven because Wardell and the board now know Meridian is fiction. The deliberate authored limit: DANIEL IS GONE. His money left at 10:41, his alarm was dead by 10:52, no badge ever logged him; the police will chase a man who was legally 'a victim' an hour ago. Let the player feel mastery over the deception, and the cold of a con artist who beat everyone and walked. Do NOT invent a capture or a lucky intercept. Nadia is wrecked; Owen is cleared but shaken; the company faces the real reckoning, fraud, not a phantom killer.

endings.title: The right answer, thinly held
endings.templateNotes: The player reaches 'he faked it and ran' without walking the full evidence path. The truth still stands and still stops the deal, but colder and less understood by the room — some execs half-believe it. Daniel is still gone. Do not invent evidence the player never found.

endings.title: Half the trick seen
endings.templateNotes: The player got one thread — that there was no murder, OR that the numbers are fraud — but not the whole. Enough to unsettle the signing and shake the murder theory, not enough to make the room certain. Suspicion may still cling to an innocent exec; the deal wobbles rather than dies. Daniel is gone regardless. Leave the gap the player left.

endings.title: You did his work for him
endings.templateNotes: The player formally names one of the executives as the murderer. An innocent colleague — Owen framed by the planted email, Devin by the visit, Simone by the argument — is fed to a homicide that never happened. The fraud stays buried, the deal dies as a murder scandal exactly as designed, and Daniel's plan works perfectly: the suspicion he manufactured lands, and he is never even looked for. The worst ending is not being wrong; it is finishing the trick for him.

endings.title: Seven o'clock
endings.templateNotes: Dawn and the signing arrive with no answer. Either the deal closes on fraudulent numbers and detonates later, or it collapses into a murder investigation with a missing body — either way the truth is buried, the executives carry the suspicion, and Daniel Serrano is a hundred miles gone under a name no one here knows.
